title: TPU v1 · Design for Test: Scan for Logic, MBIST for 24 MiB SRAM, JTAG as the Entry Point
oneLine: A 331 mm² ASIC contains 65,536 MACs and nearly 30 MiB of on-chip SRAM. Without scan, MBIST, and JTAG planned during design, manufacturing yield cannot be measured and defective parts can escape into service. DFT makes the chip manufacturable and screenable.

legend.name: JTAG / TAP (test entry)
legend.desc: The IEEE 1149.1 access port lets ATE deliver patterns, launch scan and MBIST, and use boundary scan to test pins and package connections. It is a unified external test gate—standard large-ASIC practice, not a published v1 feature.

legend.name: Scan chains (logic test)
legend.desc: Connect registers in each array PE into shift chains. Test mode shifts in known patterns, captures responses, and shifts them out for comparison, covering stuck-at and transition faults across regular MXU logic.

legend.name: MBIST (SRAM test)
legend.desc: Small built-in test engines beside the 24 MiB Unified Buffer and 4 MiB accumulators execute march read/write sequences across every cell to detect bit-cell and coupling faults. SRAM arrays require MBIST rather than scan.

legend.name: Large memories under test
legend.desc: Nearly 30 MiB of on-chip SRAM has many cells and occupies substantial area, making it defect-sensitive. Every macro needs MBIST coverage or bad cells remain invisible.

legend.name: Coverage criterion
legend.desc: DFT quality is measured by fault coverage: logic must reach defined stuck-at and transition targets, and every SRAM cell must pass the march algorithm. Insufficient coverage permits escapes and prevents credible yield estimates.

steps.cap: ① Test large logic: chain registers in every MXU PE, shift in patterns, capture responses, and cover stuck-at and transition faults.
② Test large SRAM: give the 24 MiB Unified Buffer and 4 MiB accumulators MBIST engines that run march algorithms across every cell.
③ Unify access: ATE uses JTAG/TAP to control scan and MBIST and performs boundary scan on package and pin connections.
④ Measure coverage: release only when logic targets and full-cell SRAM tests pass, enabling effective screening and yield accounting.
⑤ Close gaps: add scan points or improve MBIST whenever coverage misses target; otherwise defective parts escape to the field.

before: Understand the cost of omitting DFT: Defects are inevitable in a 331 mm² ASIC with nearly 30 MiB of SRAM. Without dedicated test logic, yield is unknowable and bad parts reach the data center. DFT starts at architecture, not after design.
Decisions to make: Fault-coverage targets, scan-chain partitioning and count, MBIST coverage, JTAG/TAP features, isolation between test and functional modes, test-pin budget, and maximum ATE time.

Required inputs: Process-library DFT cells such as scan flops, MBIST controllers, and TAP logic; defect models and fault types; block areas and SRAM inventories; and production ATE capabilities and throughput.

Hard constraints: DFT consumes area, adds scan-mux timing load, and uses pins, competing with compute (§3.3) and power (§3.9). TPU v1 publishes no exact scan/MBIST counts or coverage, so no proprietary figures are invented.
during: Divide methods by circuit type: Use scan for the MXU's vast regular logic by chaining registers and injecting patterns; use MBIST march algorithms for each SRAM, whose cells cannot be tested individually through scan.

Prioritize large SRAMs: The 24 MiB Unified Buffer and 4 MiB accumulators are defect-sensitive. Wrap every macro with MBIST that exercises reads, writes, bit flips, and coupling behavior to eliminate blind spots.

Provide one standard access path: Connect scan chains and MBIST engines to JTAG/TAP so ATE can deliver patterns, launch tests, collect responses, and inspect package connections through boundary scan.

Set coverage against cost: Higher coverage screens more faults but adds scan points, vector volume, and ATE time. Balance escape risk against test, area, and timing cost; require logic stuck-at/transition targets and full-cell SRAM march tests.

Isolate test and functional modes: Scan activity disturbs state. Ensure test mode cannot be triggered during functional operation and that inserted DFT logic does not unduly degrade functional timing.

State the disclosure boundary: Scan, MBIST, JTAG, and coverage criteria here are standard large data-center ASIC practices. Public TPU v1 material contains no proprietary chain counts, coverage values, or MBIST configuration.

result.label: Logic test
result.value: Scan chains through MXU registers (standard practice)

result.label: Memory test
result.value: MBIST for 24 MiB UB + 4 MiB accumulators

result.label: Coverage criterion
result.value: Logic stuck-at/transition targets + full-cell SRAM march

result.label: Cost
result.value: Area / timing / pins compete with compute and power

after: Verify DFT itself: Run fault simulation to confirm achieved coverage and inject memory faults to validate MBIST. Add scan points or revise the march algorithm and iterate when targets are missed.
Create production tests for ATE: Package scan vectors and MBIST sequences into an ATE program, defining test cadence and time budgets so per-device screening cost remains controlled.
Propagate constraints: Give physical implementation scan routing, TAP placement, DFT-cell area, and timing constraints; give package and board teams boundary-scan connection requirements.

Support yield and failure analysis: Feed test outcomes into yield statistics and root-cause analysis to identify defective logic or SRAM macros and inform process and design improvements. These are standard practices where v1 is unpublished.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (scan, MBIST, JTAG, and coverage details are standard large-ASIC practice, not published TPU v1 mechanisms; the case labels this distinction.)
